entities.name: Anemic Domain Model
signs: a type holds only data with accessors and no meaningful behavior
business logic that operates on a type lives in separate service-style classes rather than on the type itself
domain objects are passed around as bags of fields while decisions about them are made elsewhere

entities.name: God Class / Blob
signs: one type owns many unrelated responsibilities
a type declares an unusually large number of methods
a type spans far more lines than its peers
a type pulls in an unusually high number of imports or dependencies

entities.name: Yo-Yo Problem
signs: inheritance hierarchies that nest deeper than a few levels
understanding one type's behavior forces repeated jumps up and down a long chain of ancestors
behavior is scattered across many tiers of a class hierarchy

entities.name: Refused Bequest
signs: a subtype overrides an inherited method to throw an error or signal it is not implemented
an overridden method has an empty body that discards inherited behavior
a subtype inherits members it does not want and refuses to honor the parent contract

entities.name: Feature Envy
signs: a method reaches into another object and reads several of its accessors to do its work
a method is more interested in the data of another type than in its own
logic that belongs on one type is repeatedly performed by a method living on a different type

entities.name: Inappropriate Intimacy
signs: code navigates a long chain of member accesses across several objects to reach a value
code reaches into another object's private or internal fields from outside that object
two types know too much about each other's internal structure and rely on it directly

entities.name: God Object
signs: a single file is far longer than its peers and exposes a large number of public members
one module concentrates many unrelated responsibilities that would normally be spread across several units
the file becomes a bottleneck that almost every change has to touch

entities.name: Spaghetti Code
signs: individual functions run far longer than is reasonable to follow
control flow nests many levels deep with stacked conditionals and loops
the path through the logic is tangled enough that it is hard to predict what runs when

entities.name: Lava Flow
signs: leftover markers asking for removal or flagging temporary work persist in the code
large blocks of commented-out code are kept rather than deleted
dead or transitional code lingers long after its purpose has passed, as confirmed by its age in version history

entities.name: Copy-Paste / Duplicated Code
signs: the same multi-line block appears in more than one place with little or no variation
near-identical logic is repeated across several files instead of being shared
fixing a bug requires editing the same code in multiple locations

entities.name: Magic Numbers/Strings
signs: unexplained numeric or string literals appear directly in conditions and calculations
the same raw literal is repeated in several places with no shared name
a value's meaning is implicit and only understood by reading surrounding context

entities.name: Primitive Obsession
signs: several primitive parameters are passed together to represent a single domain concept
raw strings and numbers stand in for things that deserve their own type
validation and meaning for a concept are scattered because it has no dedicated type

entities.name: Poltergeist
signs: a class exists only to forward one or two calls to another object and holds no real state
a short-lived object's sole job is to pass a request along to a collaborator
an indirection layer adds a name without adding meaningful behavior

entities.name: Circular Dependency
signs: two modules import each other directly, forming a tight loop
a chain of imports eventually leads back to where it started
modules cannot be understood, built, or tested in isolation because they depend on each other cyclically

entities.name: Boat Anchor / Dead Code
signs: exported symbols have no consumers anywhere outside their own file
code is kept around in case it is needed someday but is never actually called
unreachable branches or unused definitions accumulate without being removed

entities.name: Golden Hammer
signs: one familiar pattern or tool is applied repeatedly even where simpler or better-fitting alternatives exist
the same heavyweight approach is forced onto problems that do not need it
the solution is chosen out of habit rather than because it suits the problem

entities.name: Big Ball of Mud
signs: individual files reach across a large number of separate directories, so the dependency graph fans out instead of flowing through clear boundaries
there is no discernible separation into layers or tiers, with presentation, business, and data concerns intermingled
the structure has grown organically without an overarching design, so almost any change ripples unpredictably through unrelated areas
entities.applies_when: a project with multiple directories and source modules present

entities.name: Vendor Lock-In
signs: platform- or vendor-specific SDK calls are made directly from business logic instead of behind an abstraction
core domain code depends on a particular cloud provider, database, or third-party service with no insulating boundary
swapping the underlying provider would require edits scattered throughout the application rather than in one isolated place
entities.applies_when: business logic that integrates with external platforms, SDKs, or services present

entities.name: Reinventing the Wheel
signs: hand-rolled implementations exist for problems already solved by mature, well-tested libraries, such as HTTP clients, date parsing, unique-id generation, or cryptography
custom code reimplements standard functionality the platform or ecosystem already provides
the bespoke implementation is harder to maintain and more error-prone than the established solution it replaces
entities.applies_when: source files implementing general-purpose utilities present

entities.name: Architecture by Implication
signs: there is no record of architectural decisions, such as a decision-record directory or any architecture documentation
the intended structure is never stated, so it lives only in individuals' heads and is inferred rather than agreed
different parts of the system follow conflicting or contradictory patterns because no shared architectural direction was set
entities.applies_when: a project of nontrivial size with multiple modules present

entities.name: Stovepipe System
signs: the same types or contracts are defined independently in multiple modules instead of shared
teams or subsystems build isolated vertical solutions that do not interoperate or share components
there are no common contracts or shared abstractions, so each silo duplicates effort and drifts apart over time

entities.name: Untested Code Path
signs: a public function or exported API has no corresponding test exercising it
error and failure paths are never run by any test, so they could break silently
conditional branches lack branch-specific tests, leaving some outcomes unverified
high-complexity logic and integration points such as external calls or file access carry no behavioral coverage
entities.applies_when: a test suite is present alongside source files

entities.name: Assertion-Free Test
signs: a test runs code but never checks any outcome
the test passes simply because nothing threw, not because behavior was verified
no expectation or assertion is made about the result of the exercised code

entities.name: Tautological Assertion
signs: an assertion compares a value to itself or checks a constant truth that can never fail
the expectation is trivially true regardless of how the code behaves
the test gives the appearance of verification while proving nothing about the system under test

entities.name: Test-Implementation Coupling
signs: tests mirror the internal structure of the code instead of asserting observable behavior
tests reach into private or internal members rather than going through the public interface
harmless refactoring that preserves behavior breaks the tests because they depend on implementation details

entities.name: Missing Negative Test
signs: only the happy path is exercised, with no tests for error, edge, or boundary cases
code that raises errors or returns failure on bad input is never tested for those conditions
invalid input, empty collections, and limit values are left unverified
entities.applies_when: a test suite is present alongside code with error or edge handling

entities.name: Flaky Test (time/order/network/fs)
signs: a test depends on the current time or clock without controlling or mocking it, so results vary by when it runs
a test passes in isolation but fails when run alongside others because it relies on shared mutable state or execution order
a test makes real network calls and breaks when a remote service is slow or unavailable
a test performs concurrent or unguarded file-system operations that race with one another

entities.name: Overly Broad Mock
signs: an entire module is mocked when only one narrow interface or function needed substituting
the mock replaces far more behavior than the test actually exercises, hiding real integration problems
broad stubbing makes the test pass even when the genuine collaboration would fail
entities.applies_when: a test suite that uses mocks or stubs is present

entities.name: Race Condition
signs: shared mutable state is read and written from concurrent paths without any guarding lock, atomic operation, or synchronization
a value is updated from an asynchronous callback or background task while another path can observe or change it at the same time
the outcome depends on the unpredictable timing or interleaving of concurrent operations, so the same input can produce different results
entities.applies_when: code uses threads, async tasks, goroutines, actors, or shared mutable state

entities.name: Deadlock
signs: multiple locks are acquired in inconsistent orders across different paths, so two flows can each hold one lock and wait forever for the other
a path waits on an asynchronous result or blocking call while still holding a lock
concurrent components form a circular wait, each blocked on a resource another holds, so progress halts entirely
entities.applies_when: code uses threads, async tasks, locks, mutexes, or message channels

entities.name: Busy Waiting
signs: a loop repeatedly checks a condition without yielding, sleeping, or awaiting, burning processor cycles while it waits
polling for a state change spins as fast as possible instead of blocking until notified
a recurring timer or polling mechanism keeps firing to check for work with no proper wake-up signal or cleanup
entities.applies_when: code uses threads, async tasks, polling loops, or timers

entities.name: Thread Starvation
signs: concurrent work is launched without any bound on how many tasks run at once, so a flood of work can exhaust the available workers
a worker or thread pool is created without a size limit, allowing unbounded growth that overwhelms system resources
long-running or blocking tasks occupy all available workers, leaving other work unable to make progress
entities.applies_when: code spawns threads, async tasks, goroutines, or uses a worker or thread pool

entities.name: God Table
signs: a single table accumulates an unusually large number of columns covering many unrelated concerns
a large share of the columns are nullable, signalling that most rows use only a subset of them
columns carry vague generic names that convey no domain meaning
one table is forced to represent several distinct entities instead of being split into focused tables
entities.applies_when: a database schema, ORM models, or data-access layer is present

entities.name: Inner-Platform Effect
signs: the application hand-builds machinery the database already provides, such as a custom query builder layered over plain queries
indexing or lookup structures are maintained in application code instead of relying on native database indexes
transaction handling is reimplemented manually rather than using the database's own transaction support
the system grows a configurable sub-platform that duplicates capabilities of the underlying engine
entities.applies_when: a database schema, ORM models, or data-access layer is present

entities.name: EAV Abuse
signs: a table stores rows as entity-identifier, attribute-name, and attribute-value triples rather than typed columns
values of many different types are all kept as generic strings, discarding type safety and constraints
querying or validating a single logical record requires reassembling it from many attribute rows
the schema is made fully dynamic to avoid ever altering tables, at the cost of integrity and query clarity
entities.applies_when: a database schema, ORM models, or data-access layer is present

entities.name: N+1 Query
signs: a query is issued inside a loop, so iterating a collection fires one database round trip per element on top of the initial fetch
related records are loaded one at a time as each parent is processed instead of being fetched together
asynchronous iteration performs a separate database call for every item rather than batching them
eager loading or a join is missing where associated data is predictably needed
entities.applies_when: a database schema, ORM models, or data-access layer is present

entities.name: Hardcoded Secret / Secret Exposure
signs: a credential-like string literal is assigned to a variable or field named like a password, token, key, or connection string
an API key, access token, or private key is embedded directly in source, configuration, scripts, or comments rather than loaded from a secret store or environment
a private key block or certificate material is committed into the repository
an environment file holding real credentials is tracked in version control instead of ignored
a secret is obscured by encoding rather than truly protected, leaving it recoverable from the source
entities.applies_when: source, configuration, or scripts that may contain credentials are present

entities.name: Injection (command/SQL/NoSQL/template/path)
signs: untrusted input is passed into a shell or process-execution call without sanitization, allowing command injection
user-supplied values are concatenated directly into a database query or filter instead of being bound as parameters
external input is interpolated into a template, prompt, or expression that is then evaluated
user input is used to build a file system path without validation, allowing traversal outside the intended directory
an unbounded user-supplied pattern is fed to a matcher in a way that can cause runaway processing
entities.applies_when: source handles user input, queries, file paths, shell commands, or templated output

entities.name: Insecure Configuration
signs: scripts or files carry overly permissive permissions such as being world-writable
a shell script that handles sensitive data omits strict failure and error-handling settings, so silent failures can leave it in an unsafe state
an automation component grants itself broad privileges or bypasses confirmation prompts when it should run with least privilege
a component that should be read-only is given unrestricted ability to run arbitrary commands
input validation is missing at a system boundary where external data first enters
entities.applies_when: configuration, scripts, or automation/agent definitions that set permissions or privileges are present

entities.name: Broken Authn/Authz
signs: an endpoint, command, or sensitive operation performs no authentication check before acting
an authorization check is missing, so any caller can reach a privileged action regardless of role
administrative credentials or a bypass flag are hardcoded, granting a backdoor around access control
session tokens or credentials are kept in an insecure location such as browser local storage or a plaintext file
protections against cross-site request forgery or server-side request forgery are absent on state-changing or outbound-request paths
entities.applies_when: source exposes endpoints, commands, or operations that require authentication or authorization

entities.name: Sensitive Data Exposure (leaky errors)
signs: error responses or messages surfaced to callers include internal details such as stack traces, query text, or system paths
secrets, tokens, or personal data are written to logs or emitted in diagnostic output
exceptions are returned verbatim to the user instead of being mapped to a safe, generic message
sensitive values are transmitted or persisted without redaction, leaving them readable in transit or at rest
debug or verbose output that reveals internal state is left enabled on paths reachable in production
entities.applies_when: source handles errors, logging, or transmission of sensitive or internal data

entities.name: Missing Lock File
signs: a dependency manifest exists but no committed lock or pin file is present alongside it, so resolved versions are not recorded
dependency resolution is left non-deterministic, allowing different exact versions to be installed at different times or on different machines
builds cannot be reproduced reliably because the exact dependency tree is never captured

entities.name: Overly Broad Version Range
signs: a dependency is declared with a wildcard or an open-ended lower-bound range that permits major version jumps
version constraints are loose enough that an incompatible or breaking release can be pulled in automatically
the same package is declared at conflicting or duplicated versions across manifests, widening the resolved range
entities.applies_when: a dependency manifest with version constraints is present

entities.name: Unused Dependency
signs: a package is declared as a dependency but is never imported or referenced anywhere in the source
type-only or companion packages are declared without the corresponding runtime dependency they support
the declared dependency surface is larger than what the code actually consumes, enlarging the attack and maintenance surface for no benefit
entities.applies_when: a dependency manifest is present alongside source files

entities.name: Known-Vulnerable / Risky Dependency
signs: a declared dependency matches a package with a known security advisory or a widely reported risky pattern
a dependency runs install-time scripts capable of executing arbitrary code as part of installation
a dependency is pulled from a non-standard or untrusted registry rather than the expected source
a dependency on the production path is outdated or unmaintained, leaving known issues unpatched

entities.name: Singleton
signs: a type prevents external construction and exposes a single shared instance through a static accessor
the same coordinated resource such as a configuration store, logger, or connection pool is reused across the whole system rather than re-created
instance creation is guarded so that repeated requests always return the one already-created object

entities.name: Factory Method
signs: a base type defines an overridable creation step and defers the choice of concrete product to its subtypes
callers obtain products through a creation method rather than naming concrete types directly with construction expressions
adding a new product variant means adding a new subtype that overrides the creation step instead of editing existing branching logic

entities.name: Abstract Factory
signs: a factory interface declares one creation method per related product type so an entire family is produced together
concrete factories each build a consistent family of matching products, keeping members of a family from being mixed
client code depends on the abstract factory and product interfaces rather than on any concrete family implementation

entities.name: Builder
signs: object assembly is split into discrete configuration steps instead of a single constructor taking many optional parameters
a separate builder accumulates the chosen options and a final build step returns the completed product
an optional director can orchestrate the steps so the same construction process can yield different representations

entities.name: Prototype
signs: new objects are produced by copying an existing instance rather than constructing from scratch
a copy operation duplicates an object's state, performing a deep copy where nested data must be independent
the system creates objects independently of their concrete types by cloning a registered example, avoiding expensive fresh construction

entities.name: Adapter
signs: a wrapper presents the interface a client expects while delegating to an existing type whose interface does not match
calls are translated from the target interface the client depends on into the calls the wrapped existing component actually supports
client code is shielded from a foreign or third-party API, depending on the desired interface rather than the concrete adaptee

entities.name: Bridge
signs: an abstraction holds a reference to a separate implementor interface rather than inheriting from a concrete implementation
the abstraction hierarchy and the implementation hierarchy can each grow new variants independently and be combined freely
what would otherwise be a combinatorial explosion of subclasses is replaced by composing one abstraction with one implementor at runtime

entities.name: Composite
signs: a single component interface is implemented by both individual leaves and containers, so clients treat them uniformly
a container holds a collection of child components and forwards operations to them, forming a recursive tree structure
clients operate on a part-whole hierarchy without distinguishing a single object from a composition of objects

entities.name: Decorator
signs: a wrapper implements the same interface as the component it wraps, forwarding calls while adding behavior before or after
behavior is added to individual objects dynamically at runtime without altering the class or affecting other instances
responsibilities are layered by stacking multiple wrappers instead of growing a single class or creating subclass variants for every combination

entities.name: Facade
signs: a single simplified entry point delegates to several internal subsystem classes on the client's behalf
clients interact with one high-level interface instead of reaching through and coordinating many low-level subsystem objects directly
the complexity and internal wiring of a subsystem is hidden behind a unified, narrower interface

entities.name: Flyweight
signs: shared intrinsic state common to many objects is stored once in a reusable flyweight rather than duplicated per object
extrinsic state that varies per use is passed in by the client at call time instead of being held by the object
a factory manages a pool of shared instances, returning an existing one when the same intrinsic state is requested to conserve memory

entities.name: Proxy
signs: a stand-in implements the same interface as a real subject and intercepts calls before forwarding them to it
the stand-in adds concerns such as lazy initialization, access control, logging, or caching around access to the real object
clients use the proxy transparently in place of the real subject, unaware of the indirection it provides

entities.name: Observer
signs: a subject maintains a list of dependent observers and notifies each of them when its state changes
observers register and unregister with the subject and implement a common update operation the subject calls
a change in one object is broadcast to many interested parties without the subject holding concrete references to their types

entities.name: Strategy
signs: a family of interchangeable algorithms is captured behind a common strategy interface with concrete implementations
a context object holds a reference to a strategy and delegates the varying work to it rather than branching internally
the selected algorithm can be swapped at runtime, and new variants are added without modifying the context

entities.name: Command
signs: a request is encapsulated as an object exposing an execute operation, decoupling the sender from the receiver that performs the action
an invoker triggers commands without knowing how the work is carried out, while a receiver holds the actual behavior
because requests are reified as objects they can be queued, logged, or reversed through undo and redo support

entities.name: State
signs: a context delegates behavior to a current state object, and the states implement a shared interface
each concrete state encapsulates the behavior for one mode and decides which state to transition to next
the object appears to change its behavior as its internal state changes, replacing large conditional blocks keyed on a state flag

entities.name: Template Method
signs: a base class defines the fixed skeleton of an algorithm in one method while deferring specific steps to subclasses
subclasses override designated abstract or hook steps without altering the overall sequence of the algorithm
the shared structure lives in one place and only the varying steps are specialized, avoiding repeated copies of the algorithm

entities.name: Iterator
signs: a separate iterator object exposes operations to advance through elements and to test whether more remain
a collection provides a factory that hands out iterators, keeping its internal storage representation hidden
clients traverse the elements sequentially without depending on or exposing how the underlying collection is structured

entities.name: Mediator
signs: a central mediator coordinates interaction between a set of colleague objects so they do not reference each other directly
each colleague communicates only through the mediator, replacing a tangled web of direct dependencies with a hub
interaction logic that would otherwise be scattered across many objects is centralized in the mediator

entities.name: Chain of Responsibility
signs: each handler exposes a handle operation and holds a reference to the next handler in a chain
a handler either processes the request or passes it along, so the sender does not need to know which handler will respond
handlers can be added, removed, or reordered in the chain without changing the sender or the other handlers

entities.name: Visitor
signs: a visitor declares a visit operation per element type, and elements implement an accept operation that dispatches to it
new operations over an object structure are added by writing new visitors instead of modifying the element classes
operations that would otherwise pollute the element classes are gathered together in one visitor, keeping the elements focused

entities.name: Memento
signs: an originator produces a memento capturing a snapshot of its internal state for later restoration
a caretaker stores and returns mementos without inspecting or depending on their contents
earlier state can be restored for undo or checkpointing while the originator's internal representation stays hidden

entities.name: Interpreter
signs: a simple language or DSL is represented as a tree of expression objects each implementing an interpret operation
terminal expressions evaluate atomic symbols while non-terminal expressions combine the results of their child expressions
evaluation walks the expression tree against a context, so grammar rules map one-to-one onto small focused expression types

entities.name: MVC
signs: responsibility is split three ways into a model holding data and business rules, a view rendering presentation, and a controller handling input
the controller updates the model in response to user input while the model notifies the view of changes so presentation stays in sync
user interface concerns and business logic vary independently because each lives in its own collaborating component rather than one tangled unit

entities.name: MVP
signs: a passive view delegates all interaction handling to a presenter rather than holding logic itself
the presenter updates the model and explicitly pushes resulting state back into the view, mediating between the two
because the view contains no decision logic, the presentation behavior can be exercised and tested independently of the user interface

entities.name: MVVM
signs: a view binds to properties exposed by a view-model so presentation state is observed rather than imperatively set
the view-model transforms model data into a shape the view consumes, with no direct reference to the view
changes propagate automatically through native data-binding so the view and underlying model stay consistent without manual wiring

entities.name: Hexagonal
signs: the application core defines port interfaces describing what it needs, and adapters implement those ports for specific technologies
the core never references concrete adapters, so user interface, database, and external services plug in at the edges without the core knowing their details
the core can be exercised in isolation by substituting test adapters for the real infrastructure behind each port

entities.name: Clean Architecture
signs: code is arranged in concentric layers from entities and use cases at the center out through interface adapters to frameworks and drivers
dependencies point only inward, so inner layers holding business rules never depend on outer layers holding technology choices
business rules survive changes to frameworks, databases, and delivery mechanisms because those volatile details sit in the outermost replaceable ring

entities.name: Event-Driven
signs: producers emit events without knowing which components will react to them
a bus or broker routes events to interested consumers that react independently of one another
components communicate asynchronously through events, enabling loose coupling, replay, and audit instead of direct synchronous calls

entities.name: Microservices
signs: the system is decomposed into loosely coupled services that can be deployed, scaled, and built with different technologies independently
each service owns its own data and exposes it only through its interface rather than sharing a database
services collaborate through well-defined APIs or messaging so a change inside one service does not ripple through the others

entities.name: Layered
signs: the system is organized into horizontal layers such as presentation, business logic, and persistence with distinct responsibilities
each layer depends only on the layer directly below it rather than reaching across or upward
separation between user interface, logic, and data access is enforced by the layering so concerns do not bleed together

entities.name: CQRS
signs: commands that change state and queries that read state are handled by separate models and handlers
the write side and read side can be optimized, scaled, or shaped independently for their differing workloads
a single overloaded model serving both reads and writes is split so each side stays focused on its own concern

entities.name: Event Sourcing
signs: application state is stored as an ordered sequence of events rather than as the current snapshot only
current state is reconstructed by replaying the recorded events from the beginning
the append-only event log provides a complete audit trail and supports temporal queries about how state looked at any past point

entities.name: Active Object
signs: asynchronous method calls are offered to callers without exposing any threading details to them
a proxy enqueues incoming requests instead of executing them directly on the caller's thread
a scheduler dispatches the queued requests for execution on a private, internally owned thread
callers and the executing thread are decoupled so invocation and execution happen separately

entities.name: Monitor
signs: multiple threads access shared mutable state through a single object that guards it
the object's methods are synchronized so only one thread operates on the protected state at a time
condition variables let threads wait for and signal state changes in a coordinated way
the locking discipline is encapsulated inside the guarded object rather than scattered across callers

entities.name: Thread Pool
signs: many short-lived tasks are executed by a fixed set of reused worker threads
a shared task queue holds work that workers dequeue and execute as they become free
thread creation and teardown overhead is avoided by reusing workers instead of spawning a thread per task
the number of concurrent workers is bounded to keep resource use predictable

entities.name: Producer-Consumer
signs: producers and consumers operate at different speeds and are decoupled through a shared buffer
a bounded buffer mediates handoff so producers and consumers never reference each other directly
synchronization on empty and full conditions blocks producers when the buffer is full and consumers when it is empty
neither side spins or polls because waiting and signaling are coordinated through the buffer's conditions

entities.name: Read-Write Lock
signs: shared data is read frequently but written infrequently and access is governed accordingly
separate read and write locks distinguish shared read access from exclusive write access
multiple readers may proceed concurrently while a writer requires exclusive access
the locking policy is encapsulated behind read-lock and write-lock operations rather than ad hoc guarding

entities.name: Repository
signs: data access is exposed through a domain-friendly collection-like interface offering operations such as find, save, and remove
the concrete implementation wraps an ORM or raw queries so persistence details stay hidden from callers
domain and business logic depend on the repository abstraction rather than directly on the database or query mechanism
callers retrieve and persist whole domain objects without writing storage-specific access code

entities.name: Unit of Work
signs: the set of new, modified, and deleted domain objects is tracked together over the course of a business operation
a single commit writes all tracked changes within one transaction rather than persisting each change separately
the tracking and transactional boundary are encapsulated so callers do not manage individual writes
multiple domain objects are persisted atomically as a single coordinated unit

entities.name: Service Locator
signs: a central registry maps service names or types to their implementations
callers obtain dependencies by asking the locator for a service type rather than constructing them directly
service lookup is centralized to avoid wiring up a full dependency-injection framework
the concrete implementation a caller receives is resolved at runtime through the registry

entities.name: Data Transfer Object
signs: data crosses a process or layer boundary as a plain object of fields with no business logic
the transfer object shields the internal domain model from external consumers
callers exchange a flat data shape rather than passing domain entities across the boundary
the object carries only the data needed for transfer and exposes no behavior

entities.name: Gateway
signs: an external system's API is isolated behind an object-oriented interface with domain-friendly methods
the implementation handles the external protocol and translation while callers stay unaware of it
callers depend on the gateway abstraction rather than on the third-party system's specifics
swapping or upgrading the external system is confined to the gateway implementation

entities.name: Message Broker
signs: producers publish messages to topics and consumers subscribe without referencing each other directly
an intermediary broker routes messages between decoupled services
communication between services is asynchronous and mediated rather than direct point-to-point calls
senders and receivers can be added or changed independently because they interact only through the broker

entities.name: Saga
signs: a distributed transaction spanning multiple services is modeled as a chain of local transactions
each step has a compensating action that undoes its effect when a later step fails
consistency is maintained without a two-phase commit by rolling back through compensations
each service owns and completes its own local step in the larger sequence

entities.name: Circuit Breaker
signs: calls to an unreliable external service are wrapped so failures can be detected and contained
the wrapper moves between closed, open, and half-open states based on tracked failures and timeouts
while open the breaker fails fast instead of repeatedly calling a service that is down
after a cooldown a half-open trial probes whether the dependency has recovered before resuming normal calls

entities.name: Monad
signs: a wrapper type holds a value together with extra context such as possible absence, failure, or side effects
a constructor lifts a plain value into the wrapper
a chaining operation sequences steps where each step takes the unwrapped value and returns another wrapped result
chained operations replace nested conditional checks for absence or failure
the chaining behavior is expected to satisfy identity and associativity laws

entities.name: Functor
signs: a container type exposes a mapping operation that applies a function to the value inside it
mapping transforms the wrapped value while preserving the surrounding container structure
the same uniform mapping interface works across different container types
mapping operations compose so a sequence of transforms can be applied in turn

entities.name: Lens
signs: a focused accessor pairs a getter that reads a nested field with a setter that returns an updated copy of the whole structure
updates to deeply nested immutable data avoid manual copying or spreading at each level
smaller accessors compose to reach deeper paths within a structure
the original structure is never mutated; setting produces a new value

entities.name: Value Object
signs: a domain concept such as money, an address, or a date range is modeled as its own type rather than a bare primitive or loose collection of fields
the type is immutable and any change produces a new instance instead of mutating the original
equality is based on the values of all fields, not on identity or reference
the type carries no identity and is interchangeable with any other instance holding the same values
validation and invariants for the concept are enforced inside the type at construction

entities.name: Entity
signs: a domain object is tracked across time by a stable unique identity rather than by its current attribute values
two instances are considered equal when their identities match, even if other attributes differ
the object's attributes may change over its lifetime while its identity remains constant
behavior and invariants that govern the object live with it rather than in external service or manager code

entities.name: Aggregate
signs: a cluster of related entities and value objects is grouped behind a single aggregate root that is the only external entry point
external code references and modifies the cluster only through the root, never reaching inner members directly
the root enforces cross-entity invariants so the whole group changes atomically as one consistency boundary
callers tell the root to perform an operation rather than reading its internals and mutating them from outside
the aggregate defines a clear transactional boundary for what must stay consistent together

entities.name: Bounded Context
signs: a large domain is split into subdomains, each with its own model where the same term can mean different things
each context owns its own model and vocabulary instead of sharing one sprawling model across the whole system
integration between contexts happens through explicit, well-defined boundaries rather than shared internal types
concepts are not forced to be identical across contexts; each context translates at its edge
models stay internally cohesive and loosely coupled to neighboring contexts

entities.name: Ubiquitous Language
signs: the same domain terms used by domain experts appear directly as names of types, methods, and modules in the code
there is no translation layer between business vocabulary and code vocabulary within a context
the shared language is defined and maintained per bounded context rather than globally
discussions, documentation, and code consistently reuse one agreed set of terms for each concept

entities.name: Anti-Corruption Layer
signs: a translation layer sits between your domain and a legacy or external system whose model differs from yours
facade, adapter, and translator pieces convert foreign concepts into your own domain language at the boundary
your core domain depends on an abstraction it owns rather than directly on the foreign system's types or protocol
the external model's quirks are prevented from leaking into and corrupting your domain model
swapping or changing the external system is isolated to the translation layer

entities.name: Specification
signs: a business rule or selection criterion is captured as its own object that can answer whether a candidate satisfies it
specifications can be combined with and, or, and not to build more complex rules from simpler ones
the same rule object is reused across querying, validation, and construction instead of being duplicated inline
new criteria are added as new specification objects without modifying existing rule logic
scattered conditional checks for the same rule are consolidated into a single named specification

entities.name: Domain Event
signs: something significant that happened in the domain is captured as an immutable event carrying a timestamp, the originating aggregate's identity, and relevant data
the event is published after a state change so other parts of the system can react
subscribers respond to the event without the source needing to know about or depend on them
behavior triggered by a change is decoupled from the code that caused the change
the event records a past occurrence and is not modified after creation
